entry.title: Repack material to Handling Unit
entry.content:
Packing Material:

Packing material is a term to represent the material which is used to pack goods for a shipment or storage. These materials required on the products for easy and proper handling during Shipping.

Examples of different type of Packaging materials include
1) cartons
2) pallets
3) crates
4) shrink wrap and other protective materials.

 

Repacking refers to the process of transferring products from their original packaging into new packaging. This process is often necessary for various reasons such as optimizing storage space, meeting customer-specific requirements, or preparing goods for shipment in a manner that improves handling efficiency.

Handling Unit: It is a container or packaging material that holds goods. H can be a pallet, carton, crate, or any other type of packaging used to handle and transport items within the warehouse and beyond.

Each Handling Unit is assigned a unique identifier (HU Number) in SAP EWM. This identifier helps in tracking and managing the HU throughout its lifecycle, from receipt to shipping.

Serial Number:

A serial number is a unique identifier that distinguishes each individual item or unit. It is used to track the specific history and attributes of that item throughout its lifecycle, from receipt to shipment and beyond.

Process to Repack material to Handling Unit:

Step1:

Go to transaction code: /SCWM/PACK

Provide the warehouse number, Work center and Storage Bin details.

 

Step2:

Navigate to the “Repack product” tab and then provide the below details

Source hu/bin

Product

Quantity

And then click on the “Serial Nos” button

Then select the material and then execute

In the new window, click on the “Assigned SNs”. Then select the Serial number and click on the execute button.

Once the serial number is added click on the execute button

Then Handling Unit is created with one material and one serial number.

Conclusion:

Process to Repack material to Handling Unit is provided.

entry.title: ABAP Cloud Application Jobs: Overcoming Key Challenges
entry.content:
Introduction

Classic batch jobs on the ABAP Application Server for S/4HANA Cloud and the BTP ABAP Environment have been replaced by application jobs. From what I could research myself, this new framework builds on the existing classical batch job functionality adding a modern layer on top and also some extensions. For ABAP developers working in cloud systems, automating tasks and setting up periodic maintenance jobs remains a valid requirement. However, the new application job framework introduces some challenges that I, too, encountered.

In this article, I’ll briefly summarize the framework, share the issues I faced while working with application jobs, and explain how I resolved them. For those interested in learning more, I’ve included links to introductory tutorials and GitHub repositories.

 

ABAP Jobs - Classic vs. New

I have tried to summarize the main features of the classic and new way to work with jobs. The most significant change, I believe, is the introduction of two new ABAP artifacts: catalog and template. Using them is straightforward. An ABAP class containing the job logic is developed and linked to a catalog. This catalog is then linked to a template, and finally the template is used for scheduling the job manually or programmatically. Catalogs also provide exit points to perform checks, set up value help for parameters, or send job notifications. The template stores input values for each application job.

Figure 1 - Classic vs. new

 

Challenges in Working with Application Jobs and Possible Solutions

Like other aspects in the ABAP Cloud, working with the new application job framework comes with some obstacles, but these are not unsolvable.

  • Adding visible application log to the application job

This is the simplest issue to solve and came up because I initially overlooked a parameter in the log-saving method. In the method save_log() of class CL_BALI_LOG_DB there is a parameter called assign_to_current_appl_job, which when set to true automatically links the log created during the run of an application job to the monitoring of the job in the Fiori app “Application Jobs”, making it visible there.

Figure 2 - How to link application log to an application job

Figure 3 - Application log for an executed job

 

  • Scheduling a background job with a technical user

Scheduling application jobs programmatically is possible with cl_apj_rt_api=>schedule_job(), but not all user types can do this. If you need a technical user to schedule jobs to avoid dialog work process timeouts, it’s not currently allowed in ABAP Cloud (as of November 2024).

To work around this, you can create an asynchronous start mechanism::

  • Instead of scheduling the job directly, the technical user adds an entry in a custom Z* table, with all the required information about the job to be started;
  • A separate periodic job (running every one minute, 10 minutes or any other interval depending on your needs), launched by a normal business user reads the above mentioned custom Z* table and starts immediately the job. This way, your job is effectively run without requiring a direct start from the technical user

 

  • Preventing multiple instances of a job from running simultaneously

A common requirement for periodic jobs is to ensure only one instance runs at a time. While in the on-premise ABAP server you could use report RSBTONEJOB2 to prevent overlap, this isn’t available in the ABAP cloud environment. However, with some code, you can implement a similar solution (source code is in the GitHub link provided):

  • First you identify the data about the current running job with: cl_apj_rt_api=>get_job_runtime_info( );
  • Then you check for other jobs running from the same catalog with: cl_apj_rt_api=>find_jobs_with_jce( catalog );
  • If another instance is running, stop the current instance to prevent overlap.

 

  • Debugging an application job

One limitation of the new framework is the inability to debug jobs. In the classic SM37 transaction, you could use the jdbg command to debug a background job. Currently, the new framework lacks this capability to the best of my knowledge.

Initially, I tried using log points to log variable contents in the background work process, but this approach failed because ABAP Cloud doesn’t allow LOG-POINT statements. Additionally, while you can add dynamic log points in Eclipse, this requires authorization for object S_DYNLGPTS, which isn’t API-released and therefore couldn’t be added to my role.

To overcome this, I created a custom variable-tracking tool. It stores variable contents in a custom table at runtime and displays them in a simple Fiori app. By adding just one line of code, developers can trace variable content at runtime:

zcl_os_ajob_watch=>trace( )->itab( name = 'INTERNAL_TABLE_DEMO' var  = itab_demo ).

zcl_os_ajob_watch=>trace( )->structure( name = 'STRUCTURE_DEMO' var  = structure_demo ).

The Fiori app shows the stored variable data per job run, with options to delete recordings as needed:

Figure 4 - Visualizing variable content saved at runtime

 

Custom Tools for a Smoother Development Process

To tackle all these challenges mentioned above, I built custom small tools with the following principles in mind:

  • They should never interfere with job execution (exceptions are caught without propagation).
  • They should use method chaining, inspired by the XCO Library, for easy implementation and removal when no longer needed.
  • They should have short, meaningful descriptions and keywords for easy usage without additional documentation.

Using these tools, the job class implementation might look like this demo code below:

 

 METHOD if_apj_rt_exec_object~execute.
   " This is a DEMO job execution - it will execute just some dummy checks
   " Set from the beginning what object/subobject and external ID you want to use for logging
   zcl_os_job_log=>set_info_for_current_session( 
       obj  = appl_log_job-obj "'ZOS_DEMO_JOB'
       sobj = appl_log_job-subobj "'JOB_LOG'
       xid  = CONV #( zcl_os_appl_job_util=>get_current_job_name( ) ) ).
   " One demo success log
   zcl_os_job_log=>log( )->add_message( 
       severity = if_bali_constants=>c_severity_information
       id       = appl_log_job-msg_class
       number   = 002 )->save_log( ).  " This is a demo success message.
   " === First I want to make sure that this job is not running already
   IF zcl_os_appl_job_util=>is_job_already_running( info_job-catalog ) = abap_true.
     zcl_os_job_log=>log( )->add_free_text(
       text = |An instance of this job is already running. Will be SKIPPED!|
       severity = if_bali_constants=>c_severity_warning )->save_log( ).
     RETURN.
   ENDIF.
   " One demo error log
   zcl_os_job_log=>log( )->add_message( 
       severity = if_bali_constants=>c_severity_error
       id       = appl_log_job-msg_class
       number   = 001 )->save_log( ).  " This is a demo error message. 
   " ==== Test saving structure content
   SELECT SINGLE FROM i_country
     FIELDS *
     WHERE country = 'RO'
     INTO (structure_demo).
   zcl_os_ajob_watch=>trace( )->structure( 
          name = 'STRUCTURE_DEMO'
          var  = structure_demo ).
   " ==== Test saving variable content
   zcl_os_ajob_watch=>trace( )->variable( 
          name = 'COUNTRY'
          var  = structure_demo-country ).
   " ==== Test saving internal table content
   SELECT FROM i_country
     FIELDS *
     INTO TABLE (itab_demo).
   zcl_os_ajob_watch=>trace( )->itab( 
          name = 'INTERNAL_TABLE_DEMO'
          var  = itab_demo ).
   zcl_os_ajob_watch=>trace( )->persist_log( abap_true ).
 ENDMETHOD.

 

 

 

Final Notes

My goal with this article was to share my journey in working with application jobs and offer ideas to improve the development process. Excellent tutorials and sample code are already available, and I’ve included links to some that I found useful, so I aimed to go beyond creating another how-to guide. My ABAP source code and Fiori app are both public on github.

I would be glad to hear any suggestions or feedback from readers!

Links and Tutorials:

  • Official SAP Help: https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-btp-abap-environment/abap-environment/application-jobs
  • Software Heroes introduction series of articles for application jobs: https://software-heroes.com/en/blog/abap-cloud-jobs-en
  • Short how-to video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YQlufhU5xXU 
  • GitHub sample code: https://github.com/SAP-samples/abap-platform-application-jobs

entry.title: Debug new for loop in ABAP 7.4 onwards syntax
entry.content:
In this blog post I wanted to share a highly effective solution for all ABAP developers to simplify debugging with the new FOR loop syntax introduced in ABAP 7.4 and later. Please refer to my solution outlined below.

Old Loop which we can easily debug in any environment step by step using /H - F5 / F6 / F7 / F8.

loop at cs_acc_posting_data-accit assigning field-symbol(<ls_accit>).
assign lt_accit_data[ awtyp = <ls_accit>-awtyp
    awref = <ls_accit>-awref
    aworg = <ls_accit>-aworg
    posnr = <ls_accit>-posnr ] to field-symbol(<ls_accit2>).
check sy-subrc is initial.
<ls_accit>-matnr = <ls_accit2>-Matnr.
<ls_accit>-ps_psp_pnr = <ls_accit2>-ps_psp_pnr.
endloop.

 

New loop GUI 7.4 and above.

lt_order = VALUE #( LET lo_conversion = NEW lcl_cfin_prg( ) IN FOR ls_cfin_accit IN lt_cfin_accit
           WHERE ( awref = <accit_ext>-awref AND
                   awtyp = <accit_ext>-awtyp AND
                   ( hkont IS NOT INITIAL OR saknr IS NOT INITIAL ) )

( ref_org      = ls_cfin_accit-aworg
  cost_center  = ls_cfin_accit-kostl
  material_no  = <accit_ext>-matnr
  chart_acc    = <accit_ext>-ktopl
  company_code = <accit_ext>-bukrs
  wbs_element  = lo_conversion->wbselement_output( lv_element = ls_cfin_accit-ps_posid ) ) ).

Method 1:

this method can also be applied in the PROD environment if method 2 is unavailable due to certain restrictions. In the above code, the latet looping technique is demonstrated, where LT_CFIN_ACCIT data is read and appended to "lt_order"  based on specific conditions.

To debug and examine LS_CFIN_ACCIT values  (the work area of LT_CFIN_ACCIT) line by line, a new method can be implemented and invoked within the same loop, as shown with the WBS_ELEMENT call in the final line.

With this approach, you can set a session breakpoint in the wbselement_output method, allowing the system to stop in this method for simplified debugging.

Note: ensure that the additional method or routine is included within this new loop.

Method 2:

 In the latest GUI version, there is enhanced funtionality for step-by-step debugging (Step Size).

I hope this solution assists you with your debugging process. Thank you!

entry.title: ABAP: Calling an API with a file attachment and parameters
entry.content:
This article will help you to set up and execute HTTPS calls to API endpoints, in this case Collibra, but it can work for other connections too.

The request was to setup a connection to Collibra, and to call an endpoint with a file attachment to create assets, then calling another endpoint with a parameter to finalize the result.

Steps:

  1. Setup the connection
  2. Create the client object
  3. Calling an endpoint
  4. Send the command and retrieve the response
  5. Calling an endpoint with parameters
  6. Adding a file as attachment with parameters
  7. Storing the result
  1. Setup the connection
    We used SM59 to setup a connection to Collibra, this has the advantage that usernames/passwords need not to be stored in the code or in parameters.
  2. Create the client object
    We used a global for this:

 

CLASS-DATA  obj_http_client TYPE REF TO if_http_client.
CONSTANTS   co_destination TYPE c LENGTH 60 VALUE 'COLLIBRA CONNECTION'.

 

And used a separate class method to create the connection:

 

 

CALL METHOD cl_http_client=>create_by_destination
      EXPORTING
        destination        = co_destination
      IMPORTING
        client             = obj_http_client
      EXCEPTIONS
        argument_not_found = 1
        plugin_not_active  = 2
        internal_error     = 3
        OTHERS             = 4.
     “ Set the properties, watch out for the protocol version, also used in SM59 setup
    obj_http_client->propertytype_accept_cookie = if_http_client=>co_enabled.
    obj_http_client->request->set_version( if_http_request=>co_protocol_version_1_1 ).
    obj_http_client->propertytype_logon_popup = if_http_client=>co_disabled.

3. Calling an endpoint
We used separate methods for each endpoint, because the call usually differs.
We are also using a prefix here, which is globally declared.

 

 

CONSTANTS   co_prefix       TYPE text60 VALUE '/rest/2.0'.

And in the method we use:

“Construct uri
lv_uri = co_prefix && '/import/results/' && [Insert Job ID here]  && '/summary'.
“Attach to client
cl_http_utility=>set_request_uri( request = obj_http_client->request uri = lv_uri ).
“See endpoint documentation regarding GET/POST etc
obj_http_client->request->set_method('GET').

 

4. Now we send out the command and capture the result, of course this is also put in a separate method:

 

    CALL METHOD obj_http_client->send
      EXPORTING
        timeout                    = 1200
      EXCEPTIONS
        http_communication_failure = 1
        http_invalid_state         = 2
        http_processing_failed     = 3.

“Communication errors can be called this way:
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      CALL METHOD obj_http_client->get_last_error(
        IMPORTING
          code    = gv_subrc
          message = gv_errortext ).

      WRITE: / 'communication_error( send )',
            / 'code: ', gv_subrc, 'message: ', gv_errortext.
      gv_error = abap_true.
      EXIT.
    ENDIF.
* Receive Result, the get_cdata will get the actual response!
    CALL METHOD obj_http_client->receive
      EXCEPTIONS
        http_communication_failure = 1
        http_invalid_state         = 2
        http_processing_failed     = 3.

    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      CALL METHOD obj_http_client->get_last_error(
        IMPORTING
          code    = gv_subrc
          message = gv_errortext ).

      WRITE: / 'communication_error( receive )',
            / 'code: ', gv_subrc, 'message: ', gv_errortext.
      gv_error = abap_true.
      EXIT.
    ENDIF.
“ Response header fields can be extracted using 
“ obj_http_client->response->get_header_fields( CHANGING fields = lt_fields ).
 " Response body is a JSON structure....
 lv_resp = obj_http_client->response->get_cdata( ).

 

5. Calling an endpoint with parameters.
Look at the ’Content_type’ that the endpoint expects!
In case of ‘application/json’ it is easy:

 

    obj_http_client->request->set_header_field( name = 'Content-Type' value = `application/json` ).
    obj_http_client->request->set_cdata( data = `{ "tagNames": [ "Hello" ] }` ).

 

In case of multipart/form data it gets more tricky. The set_cdata still can be used, but the body of the request needs to be constructed. The key to success was to press the “Code” button in Postman. This gave the solution:

 

Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=----WebKitFormBoundary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW

------WebKitFormBoundary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="finalizationStrategy"

REMOVE_RESOURCES
------WebKitFormBoundary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW--

 

The first part is the request header, followed by the request body.
In code, it looks like this:

 

    cl_http_utility=>set_request_uri( request = obj_http_client->request uri = lv_uri ).
    obj_http_client->request->set_method('POST').
    obj_http_client->request->set_header_field( name = 'Content-Type' value = `multipart/form-data; boundary=----WebKitFormROBndary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW` ).

    lv_request_body = `Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=----WebKitFormROBndary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW` && cl_abap_char_utilities=>cr_lf &&
                      cl_abap_char_utilities=>cr_lf &&
                      `------WebKitFormROBndary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW` && cl_abap_char_utilities=>cr_lf &&
                      `Content-Disposition: form-data; name="finalizationStrategy"` && cl_abap_char_utilities=>cr_lf &&
                      cl_abap_char_utilities=>cr_lf &&
                      `REMOVE_RESOURCES` && cl_abap_char_utilities=>cr_lf &&
                      `------WebKitFormROBndary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW--`.

*    " Set the request body
    obj_http_client->request->set_cdata( data = lv_request_body ).
“ Now send, get response etc

 

The boundary string needs to be a unique string, Postman will regenerate this with each call, I am using the same string over and over.

6. Adding a file as attachment with parameters.
What we need to do here is to add a multipart. Body parameters are set in the header of the multipart:

 

    DATA: lo_part TYPE REF TO if_http_entity.
    DATA: lv_raw TYPE xstring.

    obj_http_client->request->if_http_entity~set_content_type( content_type = 'multipart/form-data'  ).

    obj_http_client->request->set_method('POST').
    lo_part = obj_http_client->request->if_http_entity~add_multipart( ).
“  Set body parameters, actually stored via de header...
    lo_part->set_header_field( name = 'Content-Disposition' value = 'form-data; name="file"; filename="assets.json"' ).

    OPEN DATASET p_file FOR INPUT IN LEGACY BINARY MODE CODE PAGE '1100'.
    IF sy-subrc = 0.
      READ DATASET p_file INTO lv_raw.
      IF sy-subrc = 0.
        CALL METHOD lo_part->append_data
          EXPORTING
            data = lv_raw.
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.
    CLOSE DATASET p_file.
    lo_part->set_data( data = lv_raw ).

    lo_part->set_formfield_encoding( formfield_encoding = obj_http_client->request->if_http_entity~co_formfield_encoding_raw ).
“ Now send, get response etc.

 

7. Storing the result:
I have created a separate table (BW ADSO in this case) to store the data, using the current time stamp and a row number.
The JSON output is to be stored in name/value fields, to be able to do this, the JSON was converted using this code, which will return a simple table that can be LOOP’ed over

 

 

DATA: lv_xstring TYPE xstring,
          lt_return  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF bapiret2,
          ls_return  TYPE bapiret2,
          lv_xml     TYPE string.

    DATA(reader) = cl_sxml_string_reader=>create(
                     cl_abap_codepage=>convert_to( i_json ) ).
    DATA(writer) = cl_sxml_string_writer=>create(
                     type = if_sxml=>co_xt_xml10 ).

    reader->next_node( ).
    reader->skip_node( writer ).
    lv_xml = cl_abap_codepage=>convert_from( CAST cl_sxml_string_writer( writer )->get_output( ) ).
    lv_xstring = cl_abap_codepage=>convert_to( source = lv_xml codepage = `UTF-8` ).

    CALL FUNCTION 'SMUM_XML_PARSE'
      EXPORTING
        xml_input = lv_xstring
      TABLES
        xml_table = e_xml_table
        return    = lt_return
      EXCEPTIONS
        OTHERS    = 0.

 

 

That's all, hope you found it interesting/helpful.

entry.title: Series on Workload Analysis for HANA Platform - 4. Workload from Network, I/O and Locks
entry.content:
In the previous blog, we examined workload analysis from the perspective of HANA memory, while in this blog, we’ll focus on the workload generated by network and disk I/O, as well as the impact of lock activities, as these components are integral to overall workload analysis and can significantly influence HANA operations, maintenance, and performance by revealing potential resource bottlenecks.

Network

In the context of SAP HANA platform, "Network" refers to all communication between different processes, regardless of their physical distance or the type of connection used. This includes, but not least to, the network between application servers and HANA platform, between different nodes in high availability solutions, or between nodes within a HANA scale-out landscape. In this blog, we discussed the network more from workload analysis purpose, the following areas are usually very interesting to be discussed:

  • Network infrastructure
  • Network latency / Bandwidth
  • Communication channels
  • Buffers (HANA / Application Servers / Operating System)
  • Type of system replications
  • The table (partition) replicas or distributions
  • Network related configurations

In some cases, the network issue is more like a victim of other performance issues, e.g. locking, memory, expensive statements. Before investigating the real network related problem, we should try to eliminate the other possibilities as mentioned above. Make sure all parameters and configurations are followed with SAP standard recommendations or suggestions from cloud providers.

Workload analysis on network is a very complex topic, which should involve the knowledge not only from the HANA platform itself, but also from applications, network, operating system, cloud and hardware. So usually a batch of people will sit together to investigate and drive the network issue. But it is always important to draw the workload patterns for network first and use it for further analysis. In context of HANA, several tools can be considered to measure the network throughput and latency:

  • The ABAPmeter (to measure the network between ABAP application servers and HANA)
  • Niping tool (to thoroughly measure network latencies and bandwidths between hosts)
  • HANA monitoring views (e.g. M_SERVICE_NETWORK_IO, M_HOST_NETWORK_STATISTICS, M_SERVICE_REPLICATION, M_SERVICE_REPLICATION )
  • Cloud provider tools (SAP Monitoring service for SAP BTP, Amazon CloudWatch, Azure Network Watcher, Google Cloud monitoring)
  • SAP HANA Hardware Configuration Check Tool (HWCCT)
  • HANA database traces (e.g. indexserver trace)
  • Other useful linux tools (TCPdumps, Wireshark)

The following chart shows an example on retrieving the network throughput from HANA monitoring view. The network measurement is between nodes within scale-out landscape, and between nodes in HA environment.

Disk I/O

Although HANA is an in-memory database, monitoring disk I/O remains essential for several reasons. Since data is always persisted on disk, disk I/O occurs when data is loaded into memory or when logs are written to disk during a commit. Disk I/O is a part of HANA’s workload and can be generated by various operations. A critical disk I/O situation can severely impact overall system performance from multiple perspectives.

In HANA, the following operations may trigger I/O activities:

  • Table loading (Rowstore, Columnstore, NSE buffer)
  • Hybrid LOB / File LOBs reading
  • Savepoints
  • Commits
  • Optimize compression
  • System replication data shipping
  • Garbage collection (Persistence, LOB)
  • Backup / restore
  • Traces, etc.

If any of these configurations are inadequate, there’s a risk of excessive disk I/O consumption. To address this, it’s crucial to first understand where disk I/O originates and identify the contributing activities, enabling adjustments to reduce disk I/O consumption.

However, identifying the root cause of disk I/O isn’t always straightforward. In HANA, monitoring views such as M_VOLUME_IO_DETAILED_STATISTICS, HOST_VOLUME_IO_DETAILED_STATISTICS, and M_LOAD_HISTORY_SERVICE provide I/O information—including total consumption, I/O type (data or log), and activity count—which helps to draw workload patterns. Still, pinpointing the largest I/O contributors can be challenging, requiring indirect analysis of other symptoms.

A good starting point is examining HANA thread samples (M_SERVICE_THREAD_SAMPLES). This view reveals current operations in progress within HANA. By combining this information with I/O workload patterns, it’s possible to identify which activities may be driving high disk I/O within specific time frames. For instance, if high disk I/O is observed in a system using HANA system replication in “delta_datashipping” mode, and several threads appear in the method "system replication: data shipping" with the lock "ChannelUtilsSynchronousCopyHandler," we can deduce that the temporary high I/O is due to data replication occurring at the disk level during delta data shipping.

Another example is if high disk I/O coincides with numerous threads in the "SortRestRangeJob" method; this suggests that disk I/O is likely generated by optimize compression activities (where HANA unloads the column to disk, reloads it, and re-compresses it). To further analyze, we could consider potential causes such as a large table with frequent INSERT statements, an inefficient partitioning strategy, or parameter settings that may contribute to prolonged optimize compression activity.

Once we have identified which operations are driving high disk I/O consumption, we can gain further insight by examining additional system information, such as M_SAVEPOINTS, M_DELTA_MERGE_STATISTICS, M_BACKUP_CATALOG, and M_CS_LOADS/M_CS_UNLOADS. Due to space constraints, I won’t elaborate further here.

In general, disk I/O in HANA is unavoidable, and high I/O doesn’t necessarily indicate a problem. Understanding how these I/O activities are triggered is what truly matters. The goal is to keep disk I/O within a reasonable range to avoid impacting overall system performance.

HANA Locks

In HANA, locks often serve as indicators of performance issues, exposing problems related to aspects such as expensive statements, workload management configuration, and issues with internal HANA functions. Analyzing locks can be highly effective for diagnosing performance issues and gaining insights into workload-related challenges. For instance, a high number of record locks may indicate excessive concurrency in statements, suggesting the need for limitations. Conversely, if statement limits are set too low, many threads may end up in a waiting state, and certain internal HANA locks (e.g., mds_queueing_semaphore_free_slot_notification, BTree GuardContainer) may appear. When examining HANA workload, it’s beneficial to include blocked transactions and locking situations to gain a clearer understanding of workload patterns and performance bottlenecks.

In addition to serving as indicators of performance issues, locks can sometimes contribute to workload themselves, bringing additional CPU and memory consumption that should be minimized whenever possible. Managing locks in HANA requires extra resources to monitor, control, acquire, and release locks, particularly in situations involving lock escalation, deadlocks, or large volumes of locks. Although the resource consumption for these scenarios is usually not significant—since the effort to manage locks is minor compared to that consumed by running threads—in certain cases, locks can generate substantial workload and should be completely avoided.

Some lock information is stored in HANA heap allocators, such as transactional locks (Pool/CSRowLocking, Pool/LockManager) and row store locks (Pool/RowEngine/LockTable, Pool/RowStoreTables/LockTable). If a transaction lacks frequent commits, these heap allocators, which track acquired transactional objects and record locks, will continue to record lock information, potentially increasing memory consumption.

At times, locks can contribute to both HANA service CPU and the system CPU on the host where HANA resides. For example, spin locks (e.g., Synchronization::impl::SpinLock::lock) can sometimes be seen in the call stack of active HANA threads. These locks continuously consume CPU resources, and system CPU utilization can worsen significantly when a large number of such locks are present. We’ve also observed that non-optimized, high-concurrency statements can lead to both numerous blocked transactions and elevated system CPU usage. Call stacks and HANA runtime dumps are often invaluable for analyzing these locking situations.

Example 1 - High usage of inter-node communication due to unpractical table placement in S/4HANA scale-out landscape

In an S/4HANA scale-out landscape, an effective table distribution plan is essential. A poorly designed plan can result in excessive query execution times and increased pressure on network communication. In HANA threads, the method "PlanExecutor comm" indicates inter-node communications for distributed queries. Typically, queries are distributed when the tables involved are located on different HANA nodes. In S/4HANA, tables should ideally be grouped to reside on a single node as much as possible. Even if table distribution across nodes is unavoidable sometimes, moving one or two tables to another node can still significantly reduce inter-node communication.

Let's consider a 3-node S/4HANA scale-out system where two resource-intensive queries are running on node 3. Query 1 involves four table joins, with two tables on node 1 and two on node 3. Query 2 involves three table joins, with two tables on node 2 and one on node 3. If both queries execute on node 3 (based on HANA's weight measurement), significant amounts of data must be transferred from nodes 1 and 2 to node 3, increasing network throughput. In some cases, this type of network load is unnecessary and can be minimized.

With proper table placement (though sometimes this is challenging), moving all major tables from either node 1 or node 2 to node 3 can allow most join operations across tables to execute entirely on node 3, even if some minor joins remain distributed. This approach can improve query performance and reduce network throughput. When a significant number of tables are relocated, this can lead to substantial improvements in network throughput and overall system performance.

The following chart illustrates network throughput before and after table placement tuning. The throughput from node 1 to node 3 (red line) and from node 2 to node 3 (yellow line) significantly decreased after eliminating unnecessary cross-node table joins. If table relocation is not feasible, using statement pinning to execute a statement on a designated node can sometimes also reduce network throughput.

Example 2 - High disk I/O due to frequent access to LOB containers

LOBs (Large Objects) are columns intended for storing extensive data, such as text documents or images, and can be categorized into two types: Disk LOBs and in-memory LOBs (hybrid LOBs with a defined threshold, where LOBs below this size are loaded into memory). When accessing tables containing Disk LOBs, HANA first reads data from the LOB containers and loads it into memory (LOB page cache). This process involves disk I/O, which can be observed in threads with methods "LobGetPiece" or "LobPutPiece." By monitoring thread samples, we can identify whether high I/O situations are linked to LOB access.

In the SAP Central Finance system, the Application Interface Framework (AIF) uses XML messages to log each document processed from source systems to the Central Finance system. These XML messages are stored in the AIF table /AIF/PERS_XML, which often contains a large number of LOB files (in the DATA column), regardless of their size. High I/O activity may occur if this table is frequently accessed, and this can be confirmed by examining thread samples.

SNAPSHOT_TIME      KEY_FIGURE   SAMPLES_TOTALDETAIL    
1/12/2023 18:40THREAD_METHOD365LobGetPiece
1/12/2023 18:30THREAD_METHOD456LobGetPiece
1/12/2023 18:20THREAD_METHOD3846LobGetPiece
1/12/2023 18:10THREAD_METHOD1998LobGetPiece
1/12/2023 18:00THREAD_METHOD3465LobGetPiece
1/12/2023 17:50THREAD_METHOD16879LobGetPiece
1/12/2023 17:40THREAD_METHOD4136LobGetPiece
1/12/2023 17:30THREAD_METHOD3230LobGetPiece
1/12/2023 17:20THREAD_METHOD11245LobGetPiece
1/12/2023 17:10THREAD_METHOD2123LobGetPiece
1/12/2023 17:00THREAD_METHOD332LobGetPiece
1/12/2023 16:50THREAD_METHOD4478LobGetPiece

In the above scenario, we should consider reducing the size of LOB files, for instance, by archiving the /AIF/PERS_XML table or converting FILE LOBs into packaged LOBs. For INSERT/UPSERT/UPDATE requests, batch loading may also be beneficial. Reducing direct access to disk-based LOBs can significantly lower I/O activity in this context.

Another type of I/O activity occurs during LOB garbage collection. In earlier HANA revisions, to clean up packed LOBs in the column store, the LOB garbage collector scans all existing packed LOBs. This operation also involves I/O and can sometimes become a system bottleneck during peak business hours, similar to persistence garbage collection activities.

 

🙉🙈🙊

Series on Workload Analysis for HANA Platform

This blog post is part of the 'Series on Workload Analysis for HANA Platform'. In upcoming posts, we will demonstrate how to analyze the workload generated by HANA internal function. Here's what you can look forward to in this series:

  • 0. HANA Workload Analysis Overview
  • 1. Define and Understand the Workload Pattern
  • 2. Analyze the CPU, Threads and Numa Utilizations
  • 3. Identify the Memory Consumption
  • 4. Workload from Network, I/O and Locks (this blog)
  • 5. Performance Impact by HANA Internal Functions
  • 6. HANA Workload Management Methodology

Stay tuned as we explore these aspects in detail, providing insights and strategies to optimize your HANA environment.

entry.title: Series on Workload Analysis for HANA Platform - 5. Performance Impact by HANA Internal Functions
entry.content:
In the last blog, we discussed how network, I/O, and locks can influence HANA workloads. In this blog, we will continue by exploring how HANA's internal functions may affect performance and contribute to the overall workload.

HANA Internal Functions

The concept of HANA internal functions is somewhat abstract. For the purposes of this blog, we define internal functions as those not directly related to daily business operations but necessary for supporting the successful execution of HANA platform operations. These include, but are not limited to, functions such as merge, compression, savepoint, garbage collection, and statistics collection. Under normal circumstances, these functions run to ensure HANA operates as expected. However, there are cases where these internal functions can consume a significant portion of system resources, impacting the actual business workload running on the system.

Abnormal workloads generated by HANA internal functions can arise from various complex factors, such as impractical partitioning, improper parameter settings, or expensive queries. These workloads may appear sporadically, on a daily or hourly basis, or persist over time. It is therefore crucial to identify the symptoms of this abnormal behavior and examine each internal function's influence from different perspectives.

Here are several helpful notes addressing the behavior of specific HANA internal functions:

  • 2100009 - FAQ: SAP HANA Savepoints
  • 2112604 - FAQ: SAP HANA Compression
  • 2057046 - FAQ: SAP HANA Delta Merges
  • 2147247 - FAQ: SAP HANA Statistics Server
  • 2169283 - FAQ: SAP HANA Garbage Collection
  • 1642148 - FAQ: SAP HANA Database Backup & Recovery
  • 2502256 - FAQ: SAP HANA Caches

Again, the HANA internal function is a big topic which will not be explained in details here. In this blog, we only focus on how some of those internal functions could contribute to the HANA workload patterns and degrade the system performance.

Example 1 - High CPU consumption causing by optimize compression

Optimize compression usually be triggered after a delta merge on a specific table, to compress the data in memory. This makes it less costly for the SAP HANA database to keep data in main memory. It also speeds up searches and calculations. However, without a proper limitation on the optimize compression, this activity could use up all available threads and lead to a temporary (or last for hours!) system hang. End user could not perform their task in time manner.

The following chart shows how the optimize compression looks like on HANA thread level. This system contains 4 CPU sockets, each of them contains 54 logical CPUs. Between 1:54 and 2:28, there observed a high number of running threads in method "SortRestRangeJob". Accordingly to SAP Note 2114710, this indicates the optimize compression is working (on specific table). The trigger of this activities is usually due to a large amount of data insert into a table and triggered the delta merge.  

 

Before HANA 2.0 SPS03, there was no parameter available to directly control this behavior. To prevent such situations, the max_concurrency_hint had to be set to limit the number of job worker threads used by these internal functions. However, this could negatively affect other internal functions as well. Starting with SPS03, the parameter indexserver.ini -> [optimize_compression] -> row_order_optimizer_threads was introduced, allowing direct control over the number of threads used by the optimized compression process.

In addition to optimizing compression, delta merge activities on large tables—especially those without a proper partitioning strategy—can sometimes cause system-wide performance issues. However, these issues rarely introduce significant additional workload to the system, so we will not discuss the impact of long-running delta merges here. Since optimizing compression is often followed by these long-running delta merge activities, it is possible to modify the (automatic) merge decision function, either globally or on a table-specific basis, to ensure it runs during non-business hours, such as daily off-peak times or weekends.

Example 2 - Periodically CPU peak due to statistics server job scheduler

The statistics server is responsible for collecting historical data within HANA and storing it in the _SYS_STATISTICS schema. The statistics server is now embedded within the SAP HANA indexserver, meaning that these data collection activities contribute to the overall workload, alongside daily business operations. The STATISTICS_SCHEDULABLEWRAPPER is used to schedule collection jobs (for different monitored targets) at varying intervals. The shorter the interval, the more data is collected, which increases system resource consumption by the statistics server.

 

In a newly installed HANA instance, it is common to observe that the statistics scheduler job consumes a significant portion of the workload, often appearing at the top of the SQL list. This is normal, as no business load is running yet, so the system mainly performs internal functions, including the statistics scheduler job. However, in an established system with critical business activities, if the statistics scheduler job still accounts for more than 10% of the total workload (e.g., based on total thread consumption), this could indicate an issue that requires investigation to determine the cause of the high resource consumption by the statistics server.

The system’s historical information can be viewed through _SYS_STATISTICS.STATISTICS_SCHEDULE, which provides details on job scheduling, last run times, and job execution intervals. If significant CPU spikes occur at regular intervals, this view can help identify which statistics job is contributing most to the workload. The interval for each job can be adjusted based on its usage and criticality (with a few exceptions mentioned in SAP Note 2147247). While it is possible to disable certain collection jobs, this is not generally recommended.

Starting with HANA 2.0 SPS06, a workload class is automatically created for the statistics server. This workload class includes limits on both statement memory and thread usage. With this configuration, the workload generated by the statistics server should remain within predefined thresholds and no longer represent a significant portion of the overall system workload.

Example 3 - Surge CPU consumption by periodical scheduled garbage collection jobs

There are different types of garbage collection activities in SAP HANA. Most of these are triggered automatically and periodically by default configuration. However, when a garbage collection activity is triggered during critical business hours, it can add additional workload to the system and impact normal business operations. Sometimes, this impact can be significant. Therefore, it is important to review the settings for garbage collection jobs and ensure that all non-critical garbage collection activities are scheduled to run during non-business hours.

Persistence GC Job
The persistence garbage collection (GC) job cleans up garbage in persistence areas, and can sometimes lead to increased CPU consumption and disk I/Os. In thread samples, a large number of threads in the method "GCJob" with a running status indicates that the persistence garbage collection is active. If a significant portion of system resources is consumed by this, it is advisable to reduce the concurrency of the persistence garbage collection job by adjusting the max_gc_parallelity parameter.

LOB GC Job
The LOB garbage collection job cleans disk LOBs periodically, and its frequency is controlled by the parameters indexserver.ini -> [lobhandling] -> garbage_collect_daily_schedule_s, indexserver.ini -> [lobhandling] -> garbage_collect_interval_s, and indexserver.ini -> [row_engine] -> garbage_lob_file_handler_interval. If high CPU consumption is regularly observed during LOB garbage collection activities (e.g., thread methods like GarbageLobFileHandler, RSLobGarbageRemover, or LobGarbageCollector / LobGarbageCollectorJob), consider adjusting these parameters to ensure that collection jobs run only during non-critical business hours (e.g., 1 a.m.). For more details on scheduling LOB garbage collection at specific times, refer to SAP Note 3147465 - Schedule LOB Garbage Collection Periodically at Fixed Times.

Memory GC Job
The memory garbage collection job ensures that SAP HANA reclaims unused memory. This process helps reduce memory consumption and may also decrease the frequency of MemoryReclaim locks, which can impact business performance at times. Several parameters, as described in SAP Note 2169283 - FAQ: SAP HANA Garbage Collection, can help control the frequency and thresholds for triggering memory garbage collection jobs. If configured improperly, memory utilization may be higher than necessary. Additionally, if memory consumption becomes critical and no effective memory reclaim strategy is in place, the manual command hdbcons 'mm gc -f' can be used as a workaround to reclaim memory immediately. For more details, refer to SAP Note 1999997 - FAQ: SAP HANA Memory.

There are other types of garbage collection activities as well, but the ones discussed above are the most commonly observed contributors to additional system workload.

Above all, the workloads generated by SAP HANA internal functions are sometimes avoidable and should not be considered part of the sizing results unless these workloads are unavoidable. In a well-tuned system, the workload from internal functions should not exceed 5% to 10% of total CPU and memory resources. If this threshold is exceeded, it can lead to inaccurate assessments of the overall workload pattern and potentially result in additional costs due to unnecessary hardware resource consumption.

 
🙉🙈🙊

Series on Workload Analysis for HANA Platform

This blog post is part of the 'Series on Workload Analysis for HANA Platform'. In upcoming posts, we will demonstrate the methodology of HANA workload management. Here's what you can look forward to in this series:

  • 0. HANA Workload Analysis Overview
  • 1. Define and Understand the Workload Pattern
  • 2. Analyze the CPU, Threads and Numa Utilizations
  • 3. Identify the Memory Consumption
  • 4. Workload from Network, I/O and Locks
  • 5. Performance Impact by HANA Internal Functions (this blog)
  • 6. HANA Workload Management Methodology

Stay tuned as we explore these aspects in detail, providing insights and strategies to optimize your HANA environment.

entry.title: Series on Workload Analysis for HANA Platform - 6. HANA Workload Management Methodology
entry.content:
In the previous five blogs, we explored how to analyze SAP HANA system workload issues from various angles, including CPU, memory, NUMA, network, I/O, and HANA internal functions. With these analyses, we should have a clear understanding of what factors contribute to increased system workload or reduced overall performance. In most cases, these issues can be resolved by adjusting parameters, modifying program logic and code, or adding HANA database hints. However, some statements are difficult to optimize, and certain workload contributors can be challenging to identify. To enable the HANA system to operate efficiently and maximize performance for essential business functions, SAP HANA provides a set of approaches to manage and control workloads, helping to prevent excessive workload from impacting system performance. In this blog, we will focus on the primary methods of controlling workload in HANA, commonly referred to as "workload management."

Workload to be managed

What exactly is workload in SAP HANA? This question was thoroughly addressed in the first blog of this series. But when it comes to workload management, is it really possible and efficient to control every aspect we've previously discussed? Let’s briefly review workload in HANA and explore how to manage it effectively.

Jobs in HANA are processed by HANA threads, with each SAP HANA process, like indexserver or xsengine, comprising a set of threads that interact with the system's CPUs. To manage workload on a job level, we need to consider limiting the number of logical CPUs accessible at any given time. Several methods are available to control CPU behavior, such as parameters, workload classes, database hints, and concurrency control. Regardless of the method chosen, the primary goal is to prevent excessive CPU consumption by any single transaction.

Similarly, memory consumption is a critical factor. As an in-memory database, HANA relies heavily on efficient memory management to prevent system issues. During job execution, associated objects, buffer cache, intermediate results, and other working memory components are loaded into memory. While it’s challenging to gain a precise memory consumption for individual transactions, we can establish system-wide memory limits through parameters or workload classes to filter out requests with excessive memory demands. Additional parameters can also control the behavior of database caches, heap allocators, and working memory.

Statement timeouts and session limits are also integral to workload management. Certain parameters and workload class settings allow for timeout configurations, which prevent long-running requests from monopolizing system resources. SAP HANA’s admission control feature helps balance system workload by regulating the number of accepted sessions, akin to a traffic officer managing flow during rush hour.

HANA handles diverse types of workload, including OLAP/OLTP processes, HANA internal functions, or a blend of these. Workloads may originate directly within HANA or from external sources, such as SAP application servers or frontend applications via network connections. Each workload is tagged uniquely—by user name, job name, or application name—enabling easy identification through HANA management tools.

In this blog, we’ll primarily focus on managing CPU and memory workloads, although workload management in HANA is a broader and more complex topic.

Is workload management a MUST?

Why do we need a workload management approach for our daily business? Does it support individual transactions, or is it more restrictive? To answer this, we should first ask: Is workload management essential in SAP HANA?

The answer is both YES and NO. In an ideal world—or a "utopia" from a system administrator’s perspective—everything would operate smoothly and in order. In this scenario, all statements are optimally tuned, parameters are correctly set and regularly updated, and no unexpected issues occur due to costly statements or uncontrolled user actions (such as jobs with broad selection criteria). In this utopia, everything is orderly, and there’s no need for concern. However, in the real world, system administrators are busy managing various system challenges, working to prevent operational disruptions. Consequently, workload management becomes essential to control behaviors arising from business and end-user activities. In HANA, workload management serves as a rule set to maintain orderly operations.

In summary, workload management isn’t designed to benefit individual transactions or jobs directly; instead, it safeguards the overall system from resource bottlenecks or performance issues. At the same time, a well-tuned system creates an environment where business processes can run smoothly at optimal performance. When addressing performance issues in HANA, it is most effective to first identify and tune costly statements or configure relevant parameters, then consider applying workload management as an added layer of stability for system behavior.

HANA Workload Management Tools

HANA offers various workload management methods, each focusing on different aspects and delivering distinct impacts and outcomes. Combining these methods can also be effective and efficient. Below, we list the most common and useful tools along with their primary areas of focus.

 
Workload Management ToolsExplanation
Operating System ParametersVarious OS parameters, including those required by cloud providers, are recommended when installing the HANA platform. These settings are essential and must comply with SAP standards. Incorrect parameter configurations can lead to uncontrolled system behavior or unexpected workloads.
ABAP Stack ParametersCertain ABAP parameters also impact workload behavior on the HANA side, especially when SAP NetWeaver AS is used. For instance, the size of batch inserts can be controlled here, influencing thread usage in HANA.
ODBC/JDBC/HTTPS Connection ConfigurationsThe configuration of connection strings also affects HANA performance and behavior. For example, queries can be executed on one or multiple hosts in a scale-out landscape, and this can be controlled in the configuration.
HANA Native Parameters (Overall Workload Related)HANA provides multiple native workload-related parameters. These are crucial for managing system workload, controlling both overall statement behavior and sometimes individual requests. Refer to SAP Note 2222250 for a practical list. These parameters affect nearly all aspects of HANA, including CPU, memory, threads, I/O, network, and internal functions.
HANA Native Parameters (Others)In addition to workload-related parameters, other parameters indirectly impact system behavior, such as memory object-specific settings and garbage collection configurations. Ensuring correct configurations helps avoid unexpected performance and workload issues.
Admission ControlAdmission control helps prevent excessive requests during a given period that could result in system-wide unresponsiveness. It determines whether large volumes of requests are accepted, queued, or terminated by HANA.
Workload Classes and MappingsWorkload classes and mappings are key methods for directly managing workload by identifying tags and mapping workloads to specific rules. These classes can control CPU consumption, memory usage, and statement timeouts, especially useful for handling known resource-intensive workloads in HANA.
Active/Active Read Enable (AARE)Active/Active Read Enable is an important feature for distributing workloads between the primary and secondary sites in a high-availability (HA) environment. It allows spare hardware to reduce the CPU and memory footprint on a busy system.
Hints (ABAP, HANA, Annotations)Hints offer a precise way to control the behavior of individual statements. Some hints are workload-related, while others may improve the performance of a single query, thereby helping to reduce the overall workload.
HDBConsHDBCons provides the ability to monitor and control system performance and workload. In some cases, quick actions, such as immediate memory reclamation, can be taken using this tool to directly influence workload.
Other Tools (Crontab, Scheduler, HDBSQL, Commands)Various other tools can also impact workload. For example, optimized job scheduling can help minimize job conflicts and make better use of system resources.

Here we give three examples to demonstrate how the workload management tools work in HANA.

Example 1 - Create workload class to limit the no. of running HANA threads

At times, we observe high CPU consumption caused by a single or a group of application users. For instance, if we aggregate results from M_SERVICE_THREAD_SAMPLES by application user and set THREAD_STATE to "Running," the aggregated output can reveal that one application user consumed a significant number of running threads (over 50% of total CPU capacity) at a given point in time. Multiple running threads consumed by a single statement can lead to several performance issues, such as NUMA contention (e.g., selecting from the same table within one NUMA Node), transactional locks (e.g., massive inserts), or system resource bottlenecks (e.g., lack of available job worker threads). This occurs because the single user may execute the same request with high concurrency.

Configuring a workload class can help mitigate this issue by setting a fixed STATEMENT_THREAD_LIMIT and mapping it to the specific application user. This way, a single user within one connection will not exceed the allotted number of job worker threads, thereby reducing CPU consumption.

However, in some cases, even setting up the STATEMENT_THREAD_LIMIT may not resolve the issue, as the total CPU consumption by a single user can still be significant. This limitation arises because STATEMENT_THREAD_LIMIT only applies per connection. If the user initiates multiple connections (e.g., by scheduling multiple identical jobs simultaneously), there is no upper limit on the entire workload batch introduced by this user. In such scenarios, using TOTAL_STATEMENT_THREAD_LIMIT within the workload class can help. This setting restricts the total number of running threads (job workers) consumed by this user, regardless of the number of connections they initiate.

Before HANA 2.0 SPS06, it was not possible to set both STATEMENT_THREAD_LIMIT and TOTAL_STATEMENT_THREAD_LIMIT within a single workload class. However, as of HANA 2.0 SPS06, we can create a parent workload class and a child workload class that inherits from it. This allows the application user (or any other mapped property) assigned to this workload class to have thread limits for both single and multiple connections.

The following chart illustrates how a parent workload class can reduce CPU consumption in a busy business system. Initially, this system had only one child workload class, "WC_SLT," with STATEMENT_THREAD_LIMIT set to 8, mapped to the application user "SLT_Admin." We observed that the number of running threads for this single user could reach up to 600 (due to multiple SLT jobs running concurrently). However, after creating a parent workload class, "WC_SLT_Parent," with TOTAL_STATEMENT_THREAD_LIMIT and having it inherited by the child workload class, the total number of consumed running threads was significantly reduced.

The workload class setting is as below:

WORKLOAD_CLASS_NAMEPARENT_WORKLOAD_CLASS_NAMEPRIORITYSTATEMENT_MEMORY_LIMITSTATEMENT_THREAD_LIMITTOTAL_STATEMENT_MEMORY_LIMITTOTAL_STATEMENT_THREAD_LIMIT
WC_SLTWC_SLT_PARENT52008nullnull
WC_SLT_PARENT 5

null

null155056

Example 2 - Requests queued up by Admission Control feature

The HANA Admission Control feature allows the database to queue or reject incoming requests during critical periods when either CPU or memory usage reaches a configured threshold. For example, setting the parameter indexserver.ini -> [admission_control] -> queue_cpu_threshold to 90% causes requests to be queued once overall system CPU utilization hits 90%. This behavior is influenced by additional parameters, such as indexserver.ini -> [admission_control] -> averaging_factor_cpu and indexserver.ini -> [admission_control] -> max_queue_size. This feature proves particularly useful during times of system resource shortages.

In addition to CPU, memory thresholds can also be set. However, memory thresholds are less commonly used than CPU thresholds. This is because certain benign cases of temporary high memory usage, such as those caused by indeterminate results or page caching, can trigger Admission Control, leading to frequent blocking of requests, especially during critical business hours.

In practice, we sometimes notice that Admission Control is triggered more frequently than anticipated. In HANA load history, we rarely, if ever, observe CPU reaching the configured threshold (e.g., 90%), yet external requests are still queued or terminated. This can happen for two main reasons:

1. The granularity of CPU collection in HANA.

The detail in CPU statistics can influence the system load graph’s displayed results. The parameter located at global.ini -> [resource_tracking] -> load_monitor_granularity is set to 10,000 by default, equating to a 10-second interval. This means that SAP HANA captures CPU usage data every 10 seconds. Consequently, any spikes in CPU usage between these intervals might not be captured.

2. SAP HANA Admission Control: CPU Utilization Thresholds and Weighting Configuration

The conditions for triggering admission control are based on the CPU threshold, which by default is set to 90% for indexserver.ini -> [admission_control] -> queue_cpu_threshold. Additionally, HANA applies a weighting system to include historical statistics, allowing it to consider both past and current CPU usage. The default configured weighting parameters are as follows:

  • averaging_factor = 70
  • averaging_factor_cpu = 0
  • averaging_factor_memory = 0

The following chart provides an example of HANA database observations, showing Admission Control (AC) queued events alongside the number of running threads (job workers) over a period of time. Although CPU usage dropped below 90% of total capacity after peaking, Admission Control continued to activate, likely due to the weighted calculation. Additionally, some peak usages may not be fully captured in the thread sample results.

Example 3 - HANA Active/active Read Enable features to balance the workloads between primary and secondary node

The HANA Active/Active Read Enable (AARE) feature allows secondary nodes in an HA solution to handle a set of queries, leveraging their hardware resources. This can significantly reduce both CPU and memory consumption on the primary site while maximizing overall hardware utilization. However, there are limitations to using AARE for SQL queries; for instance, write transactions are not supported. Therefore, carefully selecting candidate queries to route to the secondary node is crucial. Generally, the most resource-intensive SELECT queries that require large intermediate results or run with high concurrency are good candidates for routing to the secondary. Nevertheless, it is always recommended to conduct a thorough review and optimization of expensive statements before implementing this change.

The following chart illustrates the impact on memory usage for both primary and secondary nodes before and after enabling the AARE feature. On the primary node, overall memory usage decreased by approximately 500 GB during both peak and non-peak business hours. This portion of memory consumption was saved and transferred to the secondary node. In this example, the secondary node's original memory usage was relatively low, presenting no risk. However, in practice, it's essential to ensure that the secondary node has sufficient memory available and to check whether table pre-load is enabled.

 

In cases where a query expected to route to the secondary node falls back to the primary instead, performing an SQLDBC trace can help identify the fallback reason. Understanding these fallback causes can be valuable in identifying the root issues behind query routing. However, we won't delve into this topic further here, as it extends beyond workload management considerations.

We've reached the end of this workload analysis series. Workload analysis is a vast topic that can't be fully covered in just a few blog posts. However, I hope this series has provided you with a solid foundation on where to start tackling workload-related issues and some essential insights. Understanding the workload patterns in your system and taking proactive measures is crucial. Not only can this improve system performance, but it can also save significant costs on hardware upgrades by reducing high workload contributors and stabilizing the system.

 

🙉🙈🙊

Series on Workload Analysis for HANA Platform

This blog post is part of the 'Series on Workload Analysis for HANA Platform' and is the last blog in this series. "I hope you’ve found this series beneficial for your HANA workload analysis journey!

The following content can help you review all the contents in this series:

  • 0. HANA Workload Analysis Overview
  • 1. Define and Understand the Workload Pattern
  • 2. Analyze the CPU, Threads and Numa Utilizations
  • 3. Identify the Memory Consumption
  • 4. Workload from Network, I/O and Locks
  • 5. Performance Impact by HANA Internal Functions
  • 6. HANA Workload Management Methodology (this blog)

Stay tuned as we explore these aspects in detail, providing insights and strategies to optimize your HANA environment.
